summary:
The correct interpretation involves understanding that a p-value less than 0.10 indicates an approximate 10% likelihood that the results are due to chance rather than a real treatment effect; this does not confirm effectiveness nor guarantee replicability or actual patient benefit. This highlights limitations inherent in relying solely on statistical significance for assessing therapeutic efficacy.

Okay, I need to summarize Agent1’s reasoning based strictly on its core clinical relevance about interpreting a p-value under 0.10 as evidence of effectiveness in treating hypertension.

First sentence should state what the p-value truly signifies clinically—specifically how it relates to randomness versus genuine effects—and clarify common misconceptions like assuming proven benefit from mere statistical significance.

Second sentence must emphasize why we cannot conclude clinical usefulness just because statistics show marginal significance—the distinction between statistical validity and practical medical impact remains critical even when thresholds appear acceptable at conventional levels.

Final summary will be exactly two concise sentences focusing only on essential clinical implications without adding external details.
